data.title: SentinelOne: Are The Stars Finally Aligning To Support Share Price Appreciation?
data.description:
A better quarter than realized-Reviewing the details

SentinelOne (NYSE:S) has not been the happiest of my recommendations. Even after a modestly positive earnings report that initially was favorably received the shares are down by about 5% over the last month and by about 19.5% year-to-date. That is just horrible relative performance during a period in which the IGV extended tech-software ETF has risen by 32% in the last year and by almost 14% on a year-to-date basis.

Owning the shares for much of the time, has been a frustrating experience. In the wake of the Crowdstrike outage a year ago, expectations for a growth inflection were high. And expectations have been high at the start of many earnings seasons. The results haven’t been horrible-but there have been scant signs of any growth inflection…until this last quarter.

The growth inflection is hiding in plain sight. By far the best metric to consider for a company such as this is net new ARR. Last quarter that metric jumped from $28 million in Q1 to $53 million in Q2. $53 million was growth of more than 20% year on year. The concomitant to that metric is RPO balance or backlog. This rose to $1.2 billion, up by 24% on a yearly basis. The increase in net new ARR was well above the level the company had anticipated. The CFO, recognized for her presentation of cautious outlooks from her days at Workday (WDAY), provided a modestly positive view of net new ARR attainment.

Our total ARR grew 24% and surpassed $1 billion, a significant milestone in our growth journey. We added net new ARR of $53 million in the quarter, which significantly exceeded our expectations. We don't provide formal ARR guidance, but you can approximate it just based on our revenue outlook that we've provided as well as the underlying business model assumptions. I would say, overall, Q2 results and the updated revenue outlook implies a relatively improved view on net new ARR for the full year.

In point of fact, the company did provide a net new ARR forecast for this year.

For fiscal year '26, we expect to deliver approximately $200 million in net new ARR, growing about 2% year-over-year. This positive trajectory builds on our reacceleration in recent quarters, continuing our growing market presence and platform adoption. At the same time, we're mindful of macroeconomic conditions, deal timing and federal spending uncertainty.

Additionally, we expect Q1 net new ARR in the low $30 million range. Our Q1 expectations include the impact of the retirement of our legacy deception solution. Excluding this impact, we would expect our net new ARR to be approximately flat year-over-year.

It was this forecast as well as the miss in Q1 net new ARR that has constrained the share price performance of Sentinel. Obviously, investors found 2% (or high single digit) growth in net new ARR after accounting for the end-of life for an acquired technology product, Deception, less than palatable. Q1 net new ARR was a miss with growth of $28 million and that precipitated another sell-off.

Our total ARR grew 24% to 948 million.

As Tomer mentioned, macro uncertainty was pronounced in April and impacted our Q1 net new ARR. This is a dynamic time, but we believe we're well positioned to navigate evolving market conditions.

Last quarter the company marginally increased its revenue forecast for the balance of the fiscal year. The increase of $2 million while net new ARR rose by $53 million reflected significant conservatism-probably justified after the number of times this company has disappointed investors over the past two years. Mathematically, if net new ARR continues to see positive trends, then revenue has to follow.

Many IT companies do not like to forecast net new ARR on a quarterly basis. The odds of forecasting quarterly bookings (essentially net new ARR is exactly correlated with quarterly bookings) correctly consistently are low. Prior to the days when most software companies had a SaaS model, every earnings season would bring some horrifying revenue miss because recurring revenues were such a small component of the total. That no longer happens of course, but that doesn’t mean that companies do not miss bookings targets. So, the question for investors considering Sentinel shares is will the company consistently achieve strong bookings growth and restore headline revenue growth to historic levels? Simply put, I believe that the company will achieve the bookings growth needed to restore stronger headline revenue growth in coming quarters.

Can Sentinel consistently achieve positive bookings results?

I don’t pretend to have second sight. My last article about Sentinel is not an example of forecast virtuosity. While the shares have risen, their appreciation of about 10% is noticeably less than the 20% appreciation of HACK, the ETF that reflects the performance of a group of cyber-security shares over the same time span. I certainly didn’t get the Q1 bookings miss right although I was concerned that tariff issues would impact sales execution.

It is about 6 months since I last reviewed Sentinel. What has changed and is the market share story starting to produce positive results? For several years, Sentinel’s performance was marked by hyper-growth-along with hyper-valuation and hyper-losses. Those attributes are all gone at this point.

Sentinel competes in several spaces within the cyber-security market. The most prominent of these is that of XDR, or extended detection and response. This part of the target is currently valued at a bit more than $7 billion with a projected CAGR of 31% as shown in the linked survey.

SentinelOne has presented its TAM in 5 categories. They include Endpoint Security, Data Analytics, Cloud Security and GenAI security as well other smaller segments. The aggregate of the TAM that Sentinel has presented is greater than $100 billion and is growing at a rate in excess of 30%/year. TAMs look great in a corporate strategy presentation, but the metric is more of a target than a specific growth metric.

SentinelOne has been a frustrating investment because it has competitive products, a far-reaching platform and a large target market. It has been spending liberally on sales and marketing. Based on those metrics the company should have been able to grow revenues faster than it has.

On the other hand, the company has been able to achieve rapid improvement in profitability metrics. In particular, the company’s non-GAAP sales and marketing growth has declined substantially. It grew by 6% year on year last quarter and it actually fell sequentially by a couple of percent. But non-GAAP sales and marketing expense was still 44% of revenues last quarter. Crowdstrike (CRWD), by comparison, had a non-GAAP sales and marketing spend ratio of 23%.

The first question to look at is whether Sentinel has enough technology advantages to restore some of its growth momentum. And the real answer to that is the merits of the Purple technology that is Sentinel’s AI focus. In the cyber security world, a Purple Team is one that brings the red and blue teams together. Sentinel’s Purple is quite a bit different than typical Purple Team functionality. (Purple Team is used by participants in the cybersecurity-space to indicate a team that combines elements of both offense and defense).

Basically, Sentinel’s Purple is designed to act as an AI analyst and a force multiplier. It prioritizes alerts, it automates investigation and response tasks and accelerating the cadence of security operations. Overall, it helps live analysts’ make sense of fragmented data and prioritize critical issues more effectively than manual processes.

These days there are an incredible number of alternative security tooling functionality offerings. It would be tedious to discuss all of them. The two most prominent competitors are Crowdstrike (CRWD) and Microsoft (MSFT) Defender. Palo Alto (PNAW) is also a leading competitor in the XDR space.

Sentinel is evaluated as a functional leader by 3rd party analysts because of its autonomous protection, its automated rollback capabilities, its operational efficiency and its unified platform. Since I have followed the company it has achieved a consistent leadership position when its offering is analyzed by the leading 3rd party evaluator in this space, MITRE ATT&CK. While it appears that Sentinel is gaining share, so too is Microsoft Defender. Sentinel needs to increase its growth in XDR as part of a strategy to positively inflect growth.

One part of the strategy has been the introduction of Flex. It is a new licensing model-fairly similar to other such models competitors have successfully introduced. It had a decent start and is enabling the company to secure larger initial deals.

At this point, some of Sentinel’s larger wins are from platform consolidation deals. The largest deals that Sentinel closes these days are based on its Singularity Cloud and its AI SIEM capability. The company continues to expand beyond endpoint with half of its bookings outside of that space for the second quarter in a row. Purple has now reached a 30% attach rate.

Purple is said to have some significant competitive advantages, the most salient of which is that it is open; it uses what is called Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework. This allows Purple to be sold as a standalone product. Just about every significant cyber-security vendor has some kind of AI capabilities. From what I can glean Purple is an “on-device” agent which means it is faster and more stable than cloud dependent models. It is “more intelligent.” It can actively reason and learn which improves the efficiency of the tool. It is capable of what is called autonomous correlation and formulating a proactive response. It is quite a bit more than an intelligent chatbot which is mainly what competitors offer at this point.

Purple achieved accelerated bookings growth last quarter and it was a record percentage of total Sentinel bookings. A significant part of the growth inflection story is a function of continuing success of Purple, both on its own and as a driver of decisions to adopt the total Sentinel platform.

A second major driver of a growth inflection for Sentinel has been its AI SIEM offering. SIEM is a major component of most cyber security architectures. It was pioneered by Splunk, but that company let the technology changes pass it by. There are many other modern interpretations of SIEM in the market. The one that Sentinel offers is schema free which basically facilitates massive data ingestion and retention at a reasonable cost and the hyper-automation that replaces the prior industry standard of Security Orchestration, Automation and Response (SOAR). Basically Sentinel AI SIEM allows for autonomous investigations and response-its major advantages are hyper-automation and data ingestion.

Lots of competitors have targeted Cisco/Splunk because the technology has become so outdated. Crowdstrike has a significant offering in the space along with Fortinet (FTNT) and Microsoft.

Sentinel recently bought a small niche vendor called Observo for $225 million. This is a company that allows users to move data freely and with nonstructured schemas. It is called data pipeline technology. It is an enabling technology that allows for the deployment of AI SIEM while migrating data from legacy installations. Observo is not going to be a significant revenue contributor in the short-term; that said, it is likely to help Sentinel close some additional AI SIEM transactions. Sentinel has described the acquisition as a way of revolutionizing Siem functionality. I think that is overwrought, but it can be a case of 1+1=3 as Observo + Sentinel's AI SIEM appears to offer users a much more efficient and cost effective way of utilizing data.

Observo was bought for 5.3 million of shares + a cash consideration of $125 million. The shares represent about a 1.5% increase to the outstanding share count.

Sentinel has also purchased Prompt Security, a vendor focused on protecting AI prompts that are frequently a target for cyber criminals. This was a $180 million purchase with a cash component of $150 million and shares equivalent to 0.5% of the outstanding shares.

Depending on the source and the precise definition, SIEM revenues overall are expected to grow in the mid-teens percent. The market size itself is estimated to be $11 billion. Currently, Sentinel has a just a small market share in the SIEM market. It is targeting for growth essentially through the displacement of Splunk deployments. The Observo acquisition is part of the overall strategy to gain market share. At least initially, adding Observo to its offering may help it secure some additional customers; Observo itself is not expected to be a significant revenue generator for at least a few quarters.

SentinelOne has gained some share and some headline customers in the SIEM space including Bloomberg, GoDaddy (GDDY) and EPAM (EPAM). I expect SIEM, particularly as part of the Singularity platform will continue to gain share and acquire larger users in years to come. SIEM is trending toward an integration with XDR and with security data lakes. That should play to Sentinel’s strength and vision.

Finally, Sentinel offers cloud data security. This is an AI powered component of the Singularity platform. It uses AI to detect threats and then encrypts and quarantines malicious files. It is part of Sentinel’s CNAPP (Cloud Native Application Protection Platform). According to Techzine Global, Sentinel’s CNAPP offering is an exceptionally high satisfaction score. This is perhaps Sentinel’s largest opportunity. 3rd party analysts size the market currently at around $50 billion and the CAGR estimate is in the mid-high teens.

Currently Sentinel is offering malware scanning, it uses AI to analyze this data for threat detection and analysis and it provides real-time automated response to perceived threats. Cloud data security is part of Sentinel’s CNAPP offering. SentinelOne’s advantages in this market are its platform (this is a real platform with appropriate integration and connectors as opposed to the faux platforms competitors have to offer). It is far more autonomous and automated than most competitors, according to 3rd party analysis. And as part of that, Sentinel’s CNAPP comes with Purple.

There really is no such thing as the “best” CNAPP anymore than there is the world’s best wine or best restaurant. Some evaluation criteria are customer specific. That said, Sentinel has gotten a significant level of market recognition-it cites 240 awards from G2 a market intelligence platform. Customer satisfaction is high according to 3rd party analysis.

Not terribly surprisingly, this is a competitive space. The two largest competitors in the space are Microsoft and Crowdstrike with shares of 26% and 18% respectively. According to the latest 3rd party analysis, SentinelOne has a 4% share, but the fastest percentage growth in the space-43%.

While certainly not without caveats, I think that Sentinel has enough technology advantages in the faster growing components of the cyber-security market to achieve a positive growth rate inflection and to continue the trend it achieved in Q2.

In the Q3-FY 2025 period, Sentinel’s net new ARR was $54 million which was about 10% growth year on year and 20% sequentially. If the company can achieve net new ARR this quarter in the range of $60 million, i.e. 10% year-on-year growth, I think it will be positively viewed by investors/analysts and set the company up to exceed its net new ARR target of $200 million for the current fiscal year. That, and a forecast for sustained percentage growth in headline sales is what is needed to get the shares to trade at better than their current compressed level in my opinion.

Sentinel’s Revenue growth challenges

In my view, the challenge to improved revenue growth is mainly one of sales execution. From my perspective, Sentinel revenue growth has been at levels well below its potential for a couple of years at this point. The company’s CRO and President is Michael Cremen who previously held a similar role at Elastic (ESTC) and before that at other prominent software companies. Mr. Cremen has been in his position 2 years. I think 22 months+ is long enough for a new CRO focused on selling the Singularity platform to large enterprises to start to show consistent results.

I felt that the goal for net new ARR for the current year of high single-digit growth on an apples to apples basis was overly cautious. While understanding the Q1 bookings miss given the environment during the worst of the tariff uncertainties, I was less than happy with the implied reduction in a full year bookings target that I thought was too low in the first place. Now with Q2 well in the rear view mirror, and what appears to be a fairly decent environment for significant transactions in the cyber security space, I am hopeful to see a pattern of consistent double digit growth in net new ARR for the company.

SentinelOne faces lots of competitors. That has been the case since the company started a dozen years ago. I have tried to present a balanced view of the company's competitive position below. Nothing that I have read suggests that there has been any recent change in the competitive landscape. I will just recapitulate the basic points of current competition:

Probably the single most significant competitor of Sentinel is Crowdstrike. The companies have somewhat different approaches to the problem of endpoint security. Crowdstrike claims to be more “cloud-native.” Sentinel emphasizes it on-device AI for autonomous protection. These days, at least at one level, Sentinel seems to be winning the argument since users are very receptive to the use of autonomous threat hunting which is delivered through hyper-automation. It would appear, at least from recent announcements, that Crowdstrike is playing catch-up.

Microsoft Defender comes with that company’s enterprise licenses so it starts with a strong position in the market. Sentinel competes by calling out its stronger performance in 3rd party tests and its autonomous threat hunting capabilities.

Palo Alto has Cortex. Palo Alto, given its overall market position and its platform approach inevitably wins some head to head competitions. From a functional point of view the 3rd party evaluators seem to favor the Sentinel approach. That is, having an Endpoint-native AI is considered the current gold standard in endpoint protection. Reviewers, including Gartner and G2 also praise Sentinel’s intuitive interface which makes it easier for smaller security teams to manage a deployment. Remediation is much easier and it has a much lower cost of data ingestion.

Having said all of that, many users are going to buy a complete Palo Alto platform regardless of the functional advantages of a company like Sentinel. To overcome that requires a strong go-to-market capability. In order to get to the next level, Sentinel has to compete at the earliest stages of a competitive evaluation-that is really what is meant by sales execution.

There isn’t some single source of truth when it comes to evaluating market share trends in this space. Obviously, Sentinel is a small fish in a big pond. It is a competitive space and the fact that Sentinel is apparently gaining share does not necessarily mean that Crowdstrike, for example, will not achieve its stated financial target. Over the years, Sentinel has been thought of as an endpoint security company. That said, recently, endpoint security has accounted for only 50% or so of Sentinel's net new ARR. According to which of the 3rd party research company's analysis one reads, the CAGR in the endpoint security space is thought to be around 10%.

But the CAGR for XDR, which is extended detection and recovery is much higher-31% according to this survey and should become the dominant endpoint security paradigm by the end of the decade. As mentioned earlier, while SentinelOne has only a 4% market share in XDR, its estimated 43% growth rate in the space is the highest amongst the 1st tier of competitors. And the reality is that is the kind of growth Sentinel ought to be able to achieve.

I do not think that Sentinel’s valuation in any way reflects its growth inflection opportunity. That is a salient part of the investment thesis and one reason why I have recently been buying the shares.

Sentinel’s business model-consistent improvements; conservative forecasts

Sentinel shares, while achieving a modest positive reaction to its earnings release and forward guidance, have traded in a narrow range, because the guidance increase was so modest. The CFO has a very long track record in terms of conservative guidance, and she basically spoke about how concerns about the macro environment and deal closures had driven the forecast.

Following a strong second quarter performance, we're raising our full year revenue outlook while maintaining prudent assumptions given the dynamic macro environment.

This outlook is supported by a strong pipeline, continued customer and partner momentum and growing contributions from our emerging products. At the same time, we are factoring in the potential for variability in the timing of larger deals.

The guide for Q3 is for sequential revenue growth of 5.8%, followed by sequential growth of 6.6% in Q4. So, there is some revenue growth reacceleration forecast, and given the track record and the current macro-environment and the company’s positively evolving product offerings, the odds are that growth will continue to exceed its forecast as it has for many quarter. That said, the revenue growth of the quarterly forecasts is 21.8% and 21.2% on a year-to-year basis, and the published 1st call consensus is for 21% revenue growth in FY 2027.

The company has acquired a technology start-up called Prompt Security. Prompt, as it name implies, is designed to protect software prompts from being hijacked by cyber-criminals. The transaction cost $180 million and will have an 80bps impact on operating margins over the next year-in other words about $8 million in annualized spending. It does have a few high profile users such as Royal Caribbean, Elastic, the New York Times (NYT) 10X Banking and Zeta Global. According to an industry analyst group, Omdia, this is a needed aggressive move to enable the company into a new field, that of securing generative AI applications in advance of a likely push by Crowdstrike to do the same. The stock portion of the transaction was 1.55 million, or 0.5% of the company’s outstanding shares.

Last quarter Sentinel continued to beat its earnings and cash flow margin projections for what is at least the 8th time in a row. As the company has improved its gross margins to near 80%, a level consistent with most software companies of this scale, the earnings beats have been coming from improving opex expense ratios and that will probably continue for at least the next couple of years. The company’s non-GAAP operating margin was 2%-its projection for this quarter is for 4%. It isn’t projecting any material change in gross margins: the comment of the CFO was that pricing discipline remained strong.

This reflects our pricing discipline and healthy unit economics while continuing to invest in services and support as we scale

Published consensus expectations are for non-GAAP EPS of $0.17 this year followed by $0.33 in FY 2027.

Some of the specifics of non-GAAP opex costs are as follows:

Revenue-Sequential Increase of 5.7%

R&D-22% vs. 21% in the year earlier period; Sequential increase of 8%

S&M-43% vs. 49% in the year earlier period; Sequential decrease of 4%

G&A-12% vs, 12% in the year earlier period; Sequential increase of 4%

Overall, as mentioned the non-GAAP operating margin rose from (3%) to 2% and rose by 400 basis points sequentially. Based on past performance, I would be surprised if the company didn’t continue to improve its non-GAAP operating margins over the next 2 quarters at some similar rates as last quarter, dependent, of course, on actual revenue performance.

SentinelOne has been generating free cash flow for some time now. It’s free cash flow margin over the 1st half of its fiscal year was about 11% compared to 11% in the year earlier period. The company does use stock based comp expense which fell from 32% of revenue to 30% of revenue. The CFO of this company has a record of focusing on SBC expense ratios, so I expect that this ratio will drop further in subsequent periods.

I generally look at share dilution as the real cost of SBC. Share dilution was 5.7% year on year, but negative last quarter. Some of the addition to the outstanding share count is because of the company’s profitability which requires the use of the Treasury Stock method in calculating outstanding shares. My models use a 2.5% share dilution over the coming 4 quarters and account for the share dilution of the recent acquisitions.

Sentinel’s Valuation-It seem mighty compressed

I think all readers have seen countless articles from a plethora of sources about the market’s over-valuation, frothiness, and bubble-like characteristics. Yes, many companies-and particularly those providing AI infrastructure have seen their valuations soar. This in turn has engendered controversies as to the reality of such projections and what kind of ROI will be realized on such staggering investments.

Of course, while valuations have been soaring, these companies have seen their orders, revenues and cash flow margins soar as well. Just the other day, Meta (META) handed out a $14 billion order to CoreWeave (CRWV) almost as though it was confetti. And the analyst team at Citi is projecting that the hyper-scalers will be spending $2.8 trillion-yes, that is trillion, by 2029 on Ai infrastructure. Of course investors have reacted to that, it is only logical to have done so. This is not an article trying to litigate that controversy. I will say that the analogs between now and the dot com bubble are totally imaginary and as Jim Cramer put it “a-historical.”

And I will acknowledge that SentinelOne shares aren’t likely to appreciate in a risk-off market such as emerged in the past 10 days or so. Market sentiment changes about as often as the weather, and I am not going to make that kind of call in this article.

That said, in my view, and strange as it may seem, Sentinel is now valued as a GARP stock. Really!

My projected EV/S for Sentinel shares is now 4.2X based on an outstanding share count of 338 million which accounts for the dilution of the two companies recently acquired. I have used a 3 year CAGR estimate of 26%-that is the company’s projected revenue growth estimate for Q4 annualized. And I have used a projected free cash flow margin of 9%, actually below the company’s free cash flow margin over the first half of the year. The combination of those metrics, which is basically how I try to value most stocks is more than 35% below average at this point.

Based on consensus estimates, the shares have a P/EG of .55X. That is a remarkable level and far below comparable P/EG ratios for most IT companies.

Why are the shares so cheap? I am not sure I can provide a facile answer. But I will try to supply a less facile answer. Part of the valuation compression is sector rotation. High growth IT stocks are not the fashion of the month, and haven’t been for some time. And they are anything but popular amongst investors on Tuesday, September 30 which finds the Wisdom Tree World Cloud ETF down by about 2.6% reversing its gain for the last 30 days of trading. Some buyers for high growth IT shares now buy only what are considered AI shares. Some believe that AI “is eating software.” There is really very limited evidence of this or none at all outside of a couple of specific companies, but that hasn’t prevented a rather grim backdrop for the share price performance of many software stocks over at least the last two months and longer.

Sentinel will never be considered an AI stock. It doesn’t sell AI infrastructure and it isn’t going to get multi-billion dollar orders from hyper-scalers to supply capacity in a space where the demand for physical capacity is significantly exceeding supply. It is pretty easy to download as many software modules as any user might want whenever they want them.

That said, Sentinel probably has the most AI centric solution set in the cyber-security market. Of course it has Purple, but almost all of its solutions now are focused on the use of autonomous agents to facilitate hyper-automation. Essentially, its entire product strategy is all about using autonomous agents to remediate potential or actual cyber-attacks. Its latest acquisitions further its strategy in terms of AI and protecting actual AI applications. What could be more AI than that?

And it impossible, at least for me, to conceive how AI applications are going to be deployed and referent processing enabled without cyber security. CNAPP, most particularly. If hyperscalers are going to spend $2.8 trillion on AI infrastructure how much will they be spending to protect that? A rhetorical question, obviously, but one that I think ought to be a prime consideration in valuing Sentinel-and many other cyber-security vendors. But that isn’t what investors are believing or betting on at the moment. There are unknowns to be sure when it comes to Sentinel. The company has to sell large deals consistently. But they have the technology and the sales capacity to do so.

I ran a DCF analysis for this report. My assumption is that Sentinel will reach a 20% free cash flow margin in 5 years. And I have used a CAGR assumption for the next 5 years at 22% with a terminal growth rate at that time of 7%. The weighted average cost of capital for Sentinel according to the linked analysis from Gurufocus is just greater than 10%. On that basis, the shares are under-valued by more than 50%. I personally do not like using DCF analysis where growth rates are elevated and the terminal growth rate is not knowable.

But just about any valuation methodology that can be reasonably employed seems to suggest that on a relative basis, Sentinel shares are materially undervalued.

Risks to the investment thesis

As a long time, but not continual holder of SentinelOne shares I can testify that there have been many false dawns and miscues here. While last quarter was seemingly a breakthrough in net new ARR, the metric of most important for me in measuring sales performance, guidance was still somewhat pedestrian and left many investors dubious about whether the quarter was an outlier or a start of a new growth chapter for the company.

I believe that sales execution is a principal risk for Sentinel. It exists in an extremely competitive space and it needs to participate in sales bake-offs for large enterprise deals. When the 3 principal competitors in the space are Microsoft, Crowdstrike and Palo Alto, the need for effective sales execution is self-evident. And the timing of the larger deals that Sentinel needs to do is notoriously unpredictable-hence the rationale for very cautious guidance that can impact the performance of the shares in the short term.

Further, the company, especially given its relative smaller scale, will be battling competitors and broader platform offerings continually. Its three larger competitors will be pitching prospective customers with the benefits of their broad platform to provide an end-to-end security solution. Sentinel these days with half of its business coming from areas beyond endpoint security is not a niche vendor, and by its nature, CNAPP essentially is a platform offering, but competition is a major competitive risk and will remain so.

Sentinel has staked out a claim as having market leading technology in the space with the development of advanced autonomous features. Staying ahead of competitors who are all trying to accomplish the same protection capabilities is a daunting task and requires resources, iteration and making the right choices. I think that will happen, and the company’s CEO Tomar Weingarten is an industry visionary. He is crucial to the success of this company.

Sentinel has made two relatively small acquisitions. There is always some risk in integrating acquisitions although the scale of these suggest that the risk is small. What is a risk is exploiting the acquired technologies to help positively inflect the company’s CAGR.

The macro environment can be a risk. Just in April, the company was unable to achieve a very modest net new ARR target because of severe, tariff induced sales headwinds. Macro conditions currently seem better than feared, but that is a risk especially given recent experience.

That said, the US government shutdown may be perceived as a risk-although typically investors look through something transitory like the shutdown.

And to reiterate, whether it should be or not, SentinelOne is a risk-on stock. If the market rotates to some other focus as has most recently been the case, SentinelOne shares will be vulnerable.

Wrapping Up: The case to buy Sentinel shares at this time and at this price

About 5 weeks ago Sentinel shares reported what I considered to be a break-out quarter with net new ARR handily exceeding the forecast. Foreword guidance was raised, although the word prudence was liberally applied and the guidance increase was quite minimal. While the shares initially appreciated modestly, they are now trading for less than they were before this latest earning release.

I believe that Sentinel has some significant and well documented technology advantages in three key areas of the cyber-security stack. This belief is a function of reports from 3rd party analysts documenting Sentinel’s competitive performance. Sentinel probably has the most pervasive and advanced use of AI amongst the prominent cyber-security vendors, and it hyper-automation and autonomous capabilities are what users indicate they are prioritizing in the space. Sentinel’s technology seems to be capable of differentiating between real threats and false positives, a key point in vendor selection.

The company, as its CFO has expressed it, has presented “prudent assumptions given the dynamic macro environment with potential for variability in the timing of larger deals.” The company introduced Flex, a more…well flexible licensing model that has been used by other vendors to help drive larger commitments. The company’s Purple AI offering is now being adopted in standalone, 7 figure deals.

The company has made two technology acquisitions, Prompt Security and Observo. Prompt will be a technology acquisition with functionality that can facilitate the safe adoption of GenAI. As the name implies, the technology is designed to safeguard the use of prompts in which bad actors try to inject malicious instructions and to leak data. Observo is an acquisition designed to further Sentinel’s ambitions in the race to stake out a significant position in the AI SIEM space.

Sentinel has continued to improve margins and free cash flow growth is accelerating. The company has a very compressed relative valuation; it is now valued in the GARP rather than growth category with an EV/S of 4.2X.

While there aren’t any scheduled investor presentations in the next several weeks, I think the relative valuation and the compressed expectations are likely to attract the attention of some analysts and institutions. While the valuation is at a level that might be attractive to P/E, the free cash flow margin is just now rising to a double digit range. In any event, the company is founder-led and I think a huge premium would be required for any transaction.

Sentinel’s next earnings report is scheduled for Dec. 6th, 2025. There have been no recent changes in estimates or ratings. The average price target is $23.45 and the shares have 23 buy ratings and 13 hold ratings. 82% of the shares are held by institutions and the short interest ratio is a moderate 5 of the float.

I have been accumulating a position in the shares. I believe that this is an appropriate time to initiate or enlarge positions for patient growth investors…if such a breed continues to exist.

data.title: Promised Recession... So Where Is It?
data.description:
Over the past three years, the economic conversation has been a “promised recession.” If you read the headlines, tracked economist surveys, or even listened to Wall Street strategists, you would have assumed a downturn was imminent. Many investors, bloggers, and YouTubers have had a “parade of horribles,” promising a recession is just on the horizon. The logic was simple enough. The Federal Reserve aggressively hiked rates from near zero, inflation spiked to four-decade highs, the yield curve inverted for the longest stretch on record, manufacturing surveys collapsed, and stocks entered a bear market in 2022. Historically, those conditions have been reliable precursors to economic pain.

And yet, here we are, late into 2025, and the U.S. economy is still standing. Not only standing, but GDP remains broadly positive, unemployment is relatively low, and equity markets sit at record highs. If the "promised recession" were near, none of that would be the case, and for many investors, the "recession that never came" has been one of the great surprises of this cycle.

But does that mean we’ve escaped it altogether? Or is the downturn still lurking, delayed by policy distortions and fiscal largesse?

I want to tackle that question today because how we answer it matters for portfolio strategy. Both the recession and the no-recession cases have merit. Each has its own probabilities, risks, and market implications.

Why a Recession Still Looks Plausible

Let’s start with the bear case.

History tells us that a recession almost always follows when the yield curve inverts; well, technically, it is when it UN-inverts. Nonetheless, since the 1960s, every sustained inversion has been followed by an economic contraction, sometimes quickly, sometimes with a lag. The inversion that began in 2022 was the deepest and longest we’ve ever experienced. If that signal still carries weight, it is logical that we should expect economic weakness to emerge.

Further, manufacturing activity has been in contraction territory for most of the past three years. The ISM Manufacturing Index, long viewed as a leading indicator, recorded 26 straight months below 50 through early 2025, briefly perked up, and then rolled back into contraction again. Historically, that kind of persistent weakness doesn’t happen in a vacuum. It usually shows up in corporate earnings, hiring, and consumer confidence.

In addition, the role of Fed tightening has been added. Monetary policy famously operates with “long and variable lags." The most aggressive hiking cycle in four decades would always take time to filter through credit markets, household spending, and corporate balance sheets. Post-pandemic distortions and massive fiscal deficits may have extended the lag, but we should expect that the effect hasn’t been repealed.

And speaking of deficits, that’s another issue. Washington has effectively been running crisis-level stimulus despite a growing economy. Federal spending has helped mask underlying weakness. But it’s also raised debt-to-GDP ratios to levels that will eventually constrain fiscal policy. The “sugar high” from deficit-financed growth is not permanent, particularly since debt detracts from economic growth in the long term.

Finally, valuations, as we discussed recently. Equity markets are priced for perfection, with mega-cap tech leading the charge. That means if growth does falter, even modestly, the downside could be amplified by the simple reality of stretched multiples.

Taken together, these factors suggest the recession call wasn’t wrong so much as early. The patient looks healthy today, but the test results show underlying conditions that can’t be ignored.

If we were to assign a probability of a recession in the next 12-18 months, it is likely somewhere around 55%.

Why the Economy Might Dodge It

Now, let’s give the bulls their due and explain why they were right to dismiss the “promised recession.”

The biggest reason we haven’t fallen into recession is simple: spending. Both consumers and the government have been more resilient than expected, and the massive amount of liquidity injected into the economy following the pandemic has created enormous distortions to economic data. The huge surge in the monetary base has not fueled the “wealth effect” in the economy but has sustained activity.

Despite higher interest rates, households benefited from excess savings built up during the pandemic, increased wealth from housing and markets, and a historically tight labor market kept nominal wages elevated. As such, people kept spending and went further into debt, which kept GDP afloat.

Meanwhile, government deficits have poured unprecedented amounts of cash into the system outside of a crisis period. Infrastructure projects, industrial policy initiatives, and entitlement spending have all provided ongoing support. In effect, Washington has been running “emergency stimulus” permanently, which has kept normal recessionary factors from occurring.

Second, the nature of the economy itself has shifted. The U.S. is far more services-driven today than in past cycles. Manufacturing weakness is notable, but it only represents about 30% of the economy today versus nearly 70% in the 70s. Given that it is a much smaller factor in the economy, it is services that we should focus on, and while weak, they have not been in recessionary territory. The chart below, an economically weighted composite of ISM Services and Manufacturing, shows that recession risks are elevated, but no recession is likely at the moment.

Third, corporate America has adapted remarkably well. Companies took advantage of ultra-low rates in 2020–2021 to refinance debt. Their balance sheets are stronger overall, and many firms have locked in cheap financing for years. That reduced the immediate pressure of higher Fed funds rates. However, such is not likely the case for smaller and mid-capitalization companies, and the risk of a rise in bankruptcies is not zero when they must refinance their debt. That could weaken economic growth but is not necessarily a guaranteed recessionary outcome.

Finally, the Federal Reserve itself has shown a willingness to pivot quickly. After hiking aggressively, the Fed began cutting in September, signaling that “risk management” and preventing unnecessary economic damage were priorities. Whether you agree with it or not, that backstop has provided psychological support to markets and businesses alike.

The bulls argue that these structural and policy supports could allow the U.S. to avoid a traditional recession altogether. Growth may slow, productivity gains (particularly from AI and automation) may cushion margins, and the expansion could grind on longer than skeptics expect.

But that is also not a guarantee, and the assigned probability of “no recession” in the next 12-18 months at 45%.

What This Means for Markets

For investors, the probabilities matter less than the preparation. Whether the economy slips into recession or not, the implication is that volatility will remain elevated, and risk management is essential. It also does not mean the financial markets can’t experience a 5, 10, or 20% correction outside the “promised recession.”

If the recession scenario plays out, equity valuations will likely compress, earnings estimates will fall, and risk assets will reprice lower. Defensive sectors, like utilities, staples, and healthcare, could outperform. Treasury bonds, ironically left for dead in 2022, would likely provide ballast as yields decline in a flight to safety.

If the no-recession scenario materializes, markets may not be “all clear” either. Corrections occur annually and can impact portfolio performance and investor psychology. With much of the “soft landing” narrative already priced in, the risk of correction is elevated. The S&P 500 is trading at multiples historically reserved for periods of strong, broad-based growth, leaving little margin of safety. Even modest disappointments could trigger corrections.

I always return to risk management here. As I’ve written many times, investing is not about making bold predictions but instead aligning portfolios to probabilities, protecting against the downside, and participating in the upside when it comes.

Today, that means remaining cautious even as markets cheer new highs. It means trimming exposure where valuations are stretched, holding a healthy allocation to cash and fixed income, and being selective in equity exposure. It means acknowledging that both outcomes—recession and no recession—are plausible and positioning accordingly.

Let’s also step back and acknowledge the broader lesson. Economists have a terrible track record at calling recessions.

  • In 2007, two-thirds didn’t see one coming.
  • In 2022, two-thirds thought one was imminent.

Both times, they were wrong. Why? Because the economy is not a machine that spits out predictable results. It’s a complex, adaptive system shaped by human behavior, policy distortions, and unforeseen shocks. Models can tell us what should happen, but a “promised recession” or not, reality often finds a way to surprise us.

That doesn’t mean we ignore the indicators. Yield curves, manufacturing surveys, and credit spreads all have information content. But it does mean we treat them as part of a broader mosaic, not as gospel.

As an investor, humility is key. The market doesn’t owe us clarity. The job is not to know the future with certainty but to navigate the uncertainty with discipline.

So forget about a “promised recession” and focus on what matters.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bitcoin: Uptober Arrived, And My New Target Price Too!
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on Bitcoin (BTC-USD). This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on November 29, 2024, and my last article about the thesis was published on July 10, 2025.

The month of September is over, and we will analyze events and perspectives for the thesis. An analysis of on-chain metrics shows that there is a clear combination of factors that can bring even higher Bitcoin prices.

What Is The Best Way To Buy Bitcoin?

Before starting data analysis, I intend to answer a frequent question in the comments: What is the best way to buy bitcoin? The answer depends on the investor's profile.

The investor who chooses to perform self-custody (through a decentralized wallet) has ample control of his wallet, but must be aware of the risks. In this model, the investor must organize to keep their wallet secure, this includes keeping the seed phrase and mitigating any theft or equipment failures.

Therefore if you are not an organized investor to maintain a seed phrase in security, or feel insecure of this modality, you should choose to invest through an ETF. Next I will describe the main ETFs.

Name Ticker AUM Sponsor Fee

iShares Bitcoin Trust

 IBIT $87 billion 0.25%
Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund FBTC $21 billion 0.25%
Grayscale Bitcoin Trust GBTC $20 billion 1.50%
Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust BTC $5 billion 0.15%
ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF ARKB $4 billion 0.21%
Bitwise Bitcoin ETF BITB $4 billion 0.20%
VanEck Bitcoin Trust HODL $2 billion 0.20%
Franklin Bitcoin ETF EZBC $0.6 billion 0.19%
Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF BTCO $0.6 billion 0.25%

If you want economy and don't care about less liquidity, you can choose Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust. However, the iShares Bitcoin Trust has the best cost benefit when we analyze liquidity and sponsor fees, in my opinion.

Context

Bitcoin rose 2.70% between September 1 and September 30, but despite the modest gains, the events were relevant. The top 100 public Bitcoin treasury companies held more than 1 million bitcoins. As a reference, the same companies had 416 thousand bitcoins the previous year.

Additionaly, a survey conducted at the end of August by BofA with Fund Managers shows that 75% have no exposure to cryptocurrencies, meaning there is a lot of space for institutional adoption and valuation of Bitcoin.

The fact is that, in general, topics such as favorable regulatory winds, institutional adoption, and changes in sentiment have already been widely discussed. Now we need to analyze what the next catalysts will be.

New Strategies In Bitcoin ETFs

One of the new catalysts, in my opinion, will be the launch of new strategies with ETFs. As new regulations are simplified, we will soon have products with Covered Call, Systematic Call Writing, Synthetic Covered Call among many others. As an example, BlackRock registered its iShares Bitcoin Premium ETF, a covered bitcoin strategy for yield investors.

This may attract new institutional and retail investors, generating greater demand that will drive the price higher, and this could extend to other cryptocurrencies. As a reference, the SEC has postponed a decision on the launch of seven ETFs until October. Among other examples, CBOE plans to launch continuous Bitcoin and Ethereum, starting on November 10th.

On-Chain Metrics

Other data that makes me optimistic are the on-chain metrics. Let’s look first at the Exchange Whale Ratio. This indicator shows the largest inflows to exchanges in relation to the total. Of course, a high value may indicate selling pressure.

As we can see, the current 0.35 levels suggest an optimistic scenario, as whales are not dominating inflows to exchanges. This is another prospective sign that makes me optimistic about the thesis.

Among other on-chain metrics, we can analyze the Active Addresses metric. The chart is below, and it is curious to note that the levels are at the same point as in 2020. This suggests that we are not in a euphoric market, which is positive in my view.

Finally, the above feeling is again corroborated by the implied volatility of Bitcoin's options. The graph below combines the Bitcoin Deribit Volatility Index and implied volatility itself, and the result is that the market expects low volatility.

In my opinion, this is the typical combination for a price breakup. We have low selling pressure (Whale Ratio), high activity network (Active Addresses) and a relatively complacent market, as we saw above. The market is silently accumulating for a breakout, and statistics are in favor of this hypothesis, as we will see.

Target Price

In all my Bitcoin articles, I bring a metric to estimate a target price for Bitcoin, and it will be no different this time. Now, let's estimate the target price through statistics, since October has been by far the best month for Bitcoin since 2012. As we can see below, we have an average return of 18.4% in the month and an 82% win rate.

By the time I write this article, Bitcoin is worth $116,777; therefore, the estimates indicate that the price could reach $138,000. Of course, past returns are not a guarantee of future returns, especially in the following month, but this will be my target for Bitcoin at the end of 2025. Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Bitcoin.

Before citing the thesis risks, I want to point out that Bitcoin is losing correlation with other assets and metrics. The chart below shows the decoupling between the price of Bitcoin and global M2 liquidity. This increasingly drives adoption by the institutional public, in my opinion, as the asset can finally be used in a risk management context for portfolios.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

Still on the theme of decoupling, we will make a Bitcoin comparison with gold. As we can see in the chart below, Bitcoin prices have decoupled from gold, and at other times, this has meant the beginning of large drops in the price of Bitcoin.

Another very sensitive data point concerns investment settlements. As we can see in the chart below, September showed the largest settlements of long positions of the year.

More intriguing is that when we expand our analysis, we see that September showed the largest sell-off among large holders since 2022. Therefore, despite the pillars, it is interesting that investors evaluate the risks of the thesis, especially regarding flow.

The Bottom Line

Bitcoin ended September with a modest high; however, there are several signs that the investment thesis is promising. There is a Bitcoin ETFs market taking more and more structured products.

Additionally, the combination of low pressure, high network activity, and a market expecting little volatility can be the perfect combination for a new ATH.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Bitcoin. Estimates suggest a target price of $138,000 at the end of 2025, and this represents an excellent return given the increasingly significant buyer flow.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: 3 Reasons I'm Not Buying The Stock
data.description:
This was me a few months ago. I live in Brazil, so I flew for 10+ hours to Omaha with the main purpose of attending the Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.B) meeting, and it was an incredible experience. I certainly wouldn't have made such an effort if I didn't really like the company and the whole philosophy that Buffett and Munger have developed over the decades.

However, although I like the company, I want to own shares in BRK.B, but I still don't have a position in the company.

That's because I try to look at things without bias. At the moment, I don't find it particularly interesting to expose myself to Berkshire Hathaway for a few reasons, such as its excessive cash position and my disagreement with the current portfolio.

First Reason: When Cash May Be Too Much

The reasons why I would invest in Berkshire Hathaway are very similar to the reasons that led me to recently initiate a position in Brookfield Corporation (BN).

These are companies with a proven track record, mainly related to capital allocation. They are companies that can access investments that I, as an individual investor, cannot, as the recent OxyChem deal showed or Brookfield's various private equity investments demonstrate. And last but not least, the ability to use insurance float to boost investment gains.

All of these reasons are very clear in Brookfield's thesis for me, but today, they are not as clear in Berkshire Hathaway, and this became clearer when I estimated the company's fair value using a sum of the parts (SOTP).

I will not address the topic of valuation now, but with that disclaimer in mind, the table below is a proxy for Berkshire Hathaway's fair market cap. The main point for now is that Berkshire's three main private segments (ex-insurance) are worth relatively little in relation to the total market cap.

Adding up the fair value of BNSF, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, and the manufacturing, services, and retailing segments, we arrive at approximately 23% of the market cap.

I believe that Berkshire Hathaway's cash pile is already well known, as it has been widely reported that the company has been accumulating cash and has even surpassed the Fed in the amount of T-Bills. And while this unlisted part of Berkshire is worth less than 25% of the market cap, the fixed income and cash part is worth 30%. Yes, one-third of the company's value is cash.

I strongly agree with this philosophy of having cash for opportunities, and I apply it. If (or when) a market crash happens, I want to have a position in Treasuries so I can buy many good and undervalued companies.

But to what extent does this cash make sense? Okay, 30% or even 50% may make sense for some, depending on their pessimism about the current market, but we are not just talking about theoretical numbers related to asset allocation but hundreds of billions.

The news of the deal for Occidental Petroleum's (OXY) petrochemical division was very relevant for both parties, and part of the reason is that this is one of Berkshire Hathaway's largest recent acquisitions. The amount? It's about $10 billion. That's not even 4% of the company's total cash.

Another example is Brookfield, which is a large company with massive investments in infrastructure, insurance, and asset management around the world. Berkshire's cash is greater than the combined market cap of Brookfield Corp., Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP).

For this reason, I don't think management is doing anything wrong; it's something more structural. In a market with stretched valuations, it is very difficult to find a destination for so much cash and so much cash flow, especially with the principle of not distributing dividends (if this policy changes, it would actually be quite easy to remedy this).

Management tries to take advantage of opportunities as they arise. One example is that the company had stakes in Nu Holdings (NU), a Brazilian fintech that has become huge and disruptive in the industry. But even with this company already having achieved a reasonable market cap, it was challenging for Berkshire to build a high position that would allow it to ride the gains in a relevant way.

To give another example, one of my positions with a high total return is a company similar to Nu Holdings but smaller, the fintech Inter (INTR). This is a company with a market cap of less than $4 billion, which means it is also virtually impossible for Berkshire Hathaway managers to build a relevant position.

In other words, the size of this cash pile seems to me to be a double-edged sword, and it is almost like a liability sometimes, as Berkshire Hathaway cannot take advantage of opportunities in small and mid-caps. In fact, even in Brookfield Corporation, which is a company with a market cap of over $100 billion, the company would not be able to gain much exposure given its size.

Meanwhile, individual investors can. In particular, I think that for this current strategy to bear fruit, there is practically only one scenario, which is a market crash. If we see a crisis in the coming years, then management would be able to be very active and generate alpha, buying giant companies with wide moats but with very compressed valuations.

Second Reason: I Don’t Like A Few Holdings Right Now

Returning to the fair market cap table for Berkshire Hathaway, one-third is cash, but another significant portion is equity investments, positions listed on the stock exchange, such as Apple (AAPL). The value of this portion is more than $300 billion, or about 26% of the company's current value.

At first glance, there is nothing wrong with this part of the business. It is as if you were investing in an actively managed investment fund, but without management fees and with an excellent track record. But even so, at the moment, I don't find it very interesting.

The main point, besides the size of the portfolio, which also ends up limiting some investments, is the main holdings. Apple, even after the divestiture, accounts for about 24% of this entire part, and as I mentioned in this article, I don't believe Apple is the most compelling company to be a shareholder in at the moment.

As much as I believe that other holdings are interesting, either as a long-term shareholder, as is the case with Amazon (AMZN), or because of the asymmetry of the valuation, as is the case with UnitedHealth Group (UNH), other positions seem uninteresting to me, such as the Coca-Cola Company (KO), which is also another very relevant position. If I dislike AAPL stock and KO stock, I am already saying that I dislike 32% of this division, which accounts for almost 10% of Berkshire as a whole.

Third Reason: The Valuation Isn’t Compelling Enough

This second reason fits in very well with the valuation issue. My dislike of Apple, or even my belief that its cash reserves are so large as to create obstacles, could be irrelevant if the valuation reflected this.

But in fact, this is not the case. Unlike other holding companies, Berkshire Hathaway is rarely traded at a significant “conglomerate discount,” due to the company’s great success and excellent capital allocation over the past decades.

Returning to the valuation table, note that my assumptions are neither overly optimistic nor pessimistic. For the private portion, such as BNSF and BHE, I assumed fair multiples in line with/slightly below peers. For the insurance portion, I assumed a fair multiple of 1.5x, for example, which is in line with what Chubb (CB) is trading at.

In the end, this all adds up to about $1.15 trillion, slightly above the current market cap. So, do I believe that BRK.B stock is undervalued? Not exactly, for a few reasons.

This is just an exercise, and I believe the stock should trade slightly below that. For example, this is considering a 0% holding company discount, and Berkshire Hathaway has some expenses related to the holding company, although they are low, with a highlight being Warren Buffett's salary as CEO, which is only $100,000 per year. Another point is that this considers the valuation of the current stock portfolio to be fair. Apple is a relevant part of this, and at the moment, it seems to me to be an overvalued company.

In other words, this exercise would point to an interesting time to buy BRK.B, if there were any relevant discounts to the price. For example, if the fair value were $1.15 trillion and the company was trading at $800 billion or $900 billion.

Another indicator that I believe is interesting is buybacks. In the chart below, you can see that even with this huge cash position and without many relevant deals to consume it, we are not seeing share repurchases, which is not a good sign from the point of view of BRK.B stock asymmetry.

The Bottom Line

Despite the reasons listed above, I do not believe it is a good time to initiate a position in Berkshire Hathaway. I do not believe the company is bad. In fact, the company seems well-positioned to deliver consistent returns.

The insurance, railroads, and BHE businesses are very solid, and although it is more difficult to maintain a consistent capital allocation with so much cash, I believe that management will be able to find uses for so many resources when we are talking about the long term. Although Buffett's retirement is a significant factor, and we should monitor how the company and the market will behave in relation to this next year, the rest of the management (such as Greg Abel) is quite competent.

Therefore, my rating for Berkshire Hathaway is a solid hold.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Shutdown Freezes Release, Not Data
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

How to assess the September jobs report in the absence of BLS data. (00:25) Apple (AAPL) drops ICEBlock app from App Store after Trump DOJ demand. (02:36) USA Rare Earth stock jumps 10% after CEO says in talks with Trump admin - report. (03:26)

This is an abridged transcript.

If the Bureau of Labor Statistics were issuing its September Employment Situation report today, economists were expecting a headline payrolls number rebounding some from August, but significantly weaker than a year ago. The unemployment rate is expected to stay at 4.3%.

CNN reported on Thursday that the BLS has collected its September labor data and is likely ready for release. That makes sense as the shutdown came just a few days before the report was due to be released. Data is generally collected the calendar week that contains the 12th of the month.

Allison Shrivastava, economist at Indeed Hiring Lab, describes the current labor market as "stagnant."

"The employment data is incredibly important, especially when we're in an economy that is very much 'low fire, low hire,' and we're really looking to any kind of indication of the direction that we're headed in," Indeed's Shrivastava said. Still, there are some private sources of data that can supplement the government data. For example, Indeed has job postings data.

She's looking at sector-level job postings to give an indication of how health care is doing vs. all other sectors. In recent months, health care has been a main engine of job creation. More than half of the jobs have been in health care and social services sectors.

The headline numbers for job postings are still above prepandemic levels, however, all but one sector have declined year-over-year, Shrivastava said. "We had a really strong economy in 2022," she noted. "But it's residual strength that's bolstering our economy now, rather than sustainable strength."

If the jobs report were distributed on Friday, she'd be looking at the diffusion, or the distribution of jobs across industries.

"There's a lot of nuances below the headline number in our economy right now, which is why paying attention to a lot of indicators is more important than ever," Indeed's Shrivastava said.

In this article on Seeking Alpha we list all the information we do know about the labor market. The link is in the show notes section.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) recently removed an app called ICEBlock, which enabled users to monitor and report the location of immigration enforcement officers, after pressure from the Trump administration.

The app was designed as a community resource to let individuals share and track sightings of immigration enforcement.

The Justice Department, led by Attorney General Pam Bondi, argued that the app endangered federal agents by exposing their activities and could increase the risk of assault against ICE officials.

"We created the App Store to be a safe and trusted place to discover apps," Apple said in a statement to NBC News on Thursday. "Based on information we've received from law enforcement about the safety risks associated with ICEBlock, we have removed it and similar apps from the App Store."

USA Rare Earth (NASDAQ:USAR) is up 7% in premarket action after jumping 23% on Thursday.

This happened after CEO Barbara Humpton told CNBC that the company was “in close communication” with the Trump administration.

“This is a field where it will not be a zero sum game,” Humpton commented regarding the rare earth supply chain. “It’s going to take a lot of players to build out this marketplace.”

“What we’re doing is keeping the administration informed of our own plans,” she said.

This comes after the government recently secured stakes in other prominent rare earth and lithium suppliers, like MP Materials (MP) and Lithium Americas (LAC).

USAR gets a quant rating of Hold on Seeking Alpha, with the highest factor grades given to growth and momentum.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Tesla (TSLA) shareholder group calls on investors to reject Elon Musk's $1T pay plan

YouTube to carry NBCUniversal's full portfolio under new long-term distribution pact

Google VP says company plans to spin off life sciences unit Verily: report

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1.3% at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.3% at $120,000. Gold is up 0.1% at $3,861.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.1%. The market in China remains closed for a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Rumble (NASDAQ:RUM) +12% - Shares jumped after the company announced a partnership with AI answer engine Perplexity to integrate AI-powered tools and boost discoverability on its video platform.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 1:30 pm Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan participates in a moderated conversation before the 10th Annual Conference of International Economics, hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, the University of Houston and the Banco de México.

  • 1:40 pm Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson speaks on "U.S. Economic Outlook and the Fed's Monetary Policy Framework" before the Drexel Economic Forum.

And finally it’s news quiz day. I’ll leave a link to the quiz in the show notes section.

data.title: Hour Loop's Economics Are Bad And Deteriorating, Clearly A Pass
data.description:
Hour Loop (NASDAQ:HOUR) is an e-commerce retailer, focused almost exclusively on the Amazon marketplace.

The company has been operating and growing inside Amazon for more than a decade, with good growth but barely breakeven profitability. It seems fairly hard to build profitability within the Amazon model, given Hour Loop's strengths.

HOUR is owned by its founders, which is good given alignment, but also negative given the low float. It might be this low float that allowed the explosive movement in the stock price in recent weeks.

Now, HOUR trades at a market cap close to $100 million despite having very low profitability, and no specific positive tailwinds ahead (rather the contrary). I believe the name is a clear Hold.

Amazon Third-Party Reseller

Hour Loop is an e-commerce retailer, buying from hundreds of manufacturers in several categories (toys, decor, garden, electronics), with over 100 thousand SKUs. The company's largest category is toys, making up 15% of sales, with no comments on other categories. This implies a wide diversification of categories and products.

The company operates almost exclusively inside the Amazon ecosystem, and has done so since its origin in 2013 (see 10-K under Business).

A Very Hard Game

As seen below, the company posted some impressive growth numbers in revenues (it IPOed in 2021), but has faced challenges in translating that revenue growth into any profitability.

I believe there is a reason for this. Competing inside the Amazon ecosystem is extremely hard, as the platform has incentives to squeeze seller margins in order to maximize its own margins and increase GMV.

It is Hour Loop itself that comments on its 10-K on several occasions that reselling is a highly competitive arena, where competitors might sometimes price below cost to boost sales, including Amazon itself competing against other sellers and selling below cost.

The business is also capital and labor-intensive, requiring the management of large inventories (inventory turns are around 3-4x), dynamic pricing, customer service, etc.

HOUR has maintained a gross margin of about 50% on its merchandise, including inbound shipping, tariffs, and duties. However, from this, it has to pay Amazon fees (which can range between 10-15% of retail), advertising (at about 5% of revenues according to the 10-K), outbound shipping, and overhead. The result is that it can only generate breakeven operating profitability.

Getting Harder

Hour Loop does not clarify in its 10-K or recent communications how much of its merchandise comes from either China or other tariffed countries, but given the categories, it is probably substantial.

This by itself is unlikely to be considered a disadvantage, given that most competitors probably source from the same countries, but as a macroeconomic trend, it will probably affect margins.

If we look ahead, the Amazon reseller model is probably going to get even more competitive, as prices from imported manufactured goods (most of what is sold on Amazon) are pressured by tariffs, and customer disposable incomes are also pressured downwards. The result is likely to be a further squeeze of seller margins in order to limit the pressure on consumers.

The Balance Sheet Is Not Strong

In general, retailing is an activity that has large cash and capital requirements. The reason is that inventories have to be purchased in advance and in bulk in order to guarantee competitive pricing and product availability.

HOUR itself lists 'Higher Capital, Low Margin' as a barrier to entry into the industry. In my opinion, the causality is the opposite; given there are no barriers, the margins and ROIC are low.

The company has been able to finance at least a portion of its inventories with payables, although this proportion has recently been decreasing, probably because of supply chain disruptions and the need to stock earlier.

Still, the company has required debt (most of it provided by the owners of the company), and has slowly 'eaten' the cash reserves that it received on its IPO (2021).

This puts the company in a relatively weak position if the economy cools down and competition leads to the company's operating margins dipping into negative territory.

Controlled Company

HOUR is almost exclusively owned by its founder, CEO, CFO, and chairman (all the same person), and his wife (a VP at the company). The couple owns 94.9% of all shares outstanding.

This is a positive when read from the perspective of alignment of interest. The people who are more interested in HOUR doing well are arguably the ones who own a significant stake in the company, and would therefore benefit the most. These people are in charge of the company.

However, given the level of control, the situation presents a negative in the lack of float (5% or less of the company), which leads to extremely low liquidity and high trading as a percentage of float.

As seen below, when we remove the two volume-spike events of January and September (more below), the company's average daily volumes were 150 thousand shares, or about $225 thousand at the then prevailing prices. This average is highly skewed by some smaller spike events, given that most observations are below the average. This low liquidity still represents almost 10% of the float (less than 2 million shares) daily.

This lack of liquidity is what allows the recent price spikes, which can be considered meme-like. When volumes increase significantly, the prices become very volatile. We can observe below how, during some specific spike days, volumes reached more than 200 million shares a day, or 100x the company's float, leading to 300%+ appreciation in a single day.

This risk is enormous for both short and long positions, given that the stock can move up or down extremely violently without any fundamental information change.

Valuation Is Totally Unattractive

Recapping the business, Hour Loop is a company that has grown a lot, albeit slowing down in recent years post-IPO, given the challenges coming from the post-pandemic and supply chain disruptions.

Despite this growth, it has barely ever become a profitable operation, given that it competes in a sub-industry that has very challenging economics, where competition is fierce and the incentives of the platform owner (Amazon) are not necessarily coincident, and sometimes even adversarial, with those of the third-party sellers.

The company is now facing a challenging, risky, and uncertain outlook, with pricing pressures coming from tariffs, added to a cooling economy with lower disposable income. The result is most likely going to be pressure margin, as sellers and Amazon try to squeeze margins to keep GMVs up.

HOUR does not have a strong balance sheet to resist this kind of trend, slowly losing its cash and securities to a level that is relatively low today (albeit affected by seasonal factors as inventories grow before the holidays).

In this context, HOUR trading at a $100 million market cap is difficult to justify. Even assuming the company reaches 'high' historical margins of 2-3%, it would only generate $3-4 million in operating income ($2-3 million in net income after taxes) from revenues of $135 million.

That is, the company operates in a low-profitability business, is unprofitable or barely breakeven, faces a challenging context, has low cash reserves, but trades at 30x+ optimistic earnings. That seems like an easy pass to me, and therefore I initiate HOUR with a Hold rating.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Is Primed For A Post-Earnings Recovery (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
My Thesis Update

Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF)(NVON:CA) is a ~$255 billion market-cap pharma company from Denmark, with a focus on treating diabetes, obesity, and different types of rare diseases, so it's basically positioned in the fastest-growing and seemingly non-cyclical niches of the pharmaceutical industry (unlike Pfizer (PFE) or Moderna (MRNA), which were hit by post-COVID sales plunges in the past). Anyway, when I looked at NVO back in July 2025, I thought that the sell-off at the time was already largely overdone. I was a bit premature in my call; now I see it when the stock went another 15-20% lower before its recent attempt to finally recover (the stock is still down over 13% since my bullish call, though).

Despite the continued weakness in NVO's price, I'm not losing hope and actually think that the stock's recent attempt to recover is only the beginning of a prolonged and sustained bullish comeback. Novo Nordisk just needs a catalyst to run, and I view its upcoming Q3 FY2025 earnings print to be the exact thing needed to revive bulls' hope and let the current valuation discount gradually reduce. Let me explain why.

Novo Nordisk's Q2 2025 Should Catalyze The Comeback Rally

The firm is set to report its Q3 earnings in about a month, but banks are already busy writing their previews, so I decided to follow suit and write my own one today. But before coming to the guidance and forward projections, let's take a look at NVO's recent past.

The Q2 came out in early August, and it was after my first and only piece on NVO, so I have to make a brief review first. The revenues of DKK 76.9 billion, or $12 billion beat the consensus by a bit over $45 million, or 0.38%, rising by ~20.48% on a YoY basis for the quarter. Unfortunately, NVO slightly missed the bottom-line consensus (by 0.45%) after 3 straight quarters of strong beating. On the other hand, Novo always had difficulties in meeting the market's second-quarter expectations: In 2023 and 2024, the misses were 5.82% and 9.52%, respectively, according to Seeking Alpha, so I'd consider the FY2025 miss to be actually "better-than-historic".

Anyway, a miss is a miss, and I shouldn't make any justifications for it. And Novo had its reasons for the YTD fall, although it might seem like the business momentum doesn't agree.

The meltdown of the past rally came as a result of lowered 2025 sales and profit guidance, which the market then superimposed on the rising competition from Eli Lilly (LLY) and the widespread availability of unbranded, often unapproved versions of GLP-1 drugs in the U.S. All that happened amid the rising concerns of the drug tariffs, so NVO's previously high valuation multiples weighed heavily on the price, and we saw what we saw.

Now it's important what comes next after the ~60% off-peak collapse that Novo went through.

Facing the above-mentioned headwinds, the management seems to have moved quickly to adapt, and the most notable change happened at the very top. Maziar Mike Doustdar, formerly EVP of International Operations, has taken over as CEO, replacing Lars Fruergaard Jørgensen, who clearly lost control of the ongoing erosion of the firm's market share (particularly in the US). Now with Doustdar at the helm, I think Novo will be focusing more on international non-US selling markets where they still have the needed pricing power. Also, the most logical thing for the CEO is to cut operating costs in anticipation of the lost US share, and if so, all that can bring in some level of margin protection.

Goldman Sachs analysts (proprietary source, September 2025) wrote in their preview note to clients that they expect Novo's U.S. Ozempic sales to be flat on a YoY basis, with "ex-U.S. growth offsetting domestic weakness." For Wegovy, they modeled a 14% decline in net price per prescription "due to a mix shift toward lower-priced channels," but the sales in that segment should increase by 21% YoY (in CER currency) anyway.
